title: The Difficulty of Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Difficulty of Evangelion             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 4:58 pm Personally, I don't find Eva that "hard" to understand, philosophically, psychologically, and lore-wise, but the main complaint from haters is that it's far too confusing. I can see why, but never had any problems with it myself. What do you think the difficulty of Evangelion stems from?

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Wed Jul 14, 2010 5:12 pm It comes from all the misinformation spread in Eva 's early days that won't die because people immediately access it when trying to understand the show better. Their perception of the show is then skewed through what they've read, resulting in more confusion.

[]:             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 5:15 pm Good point. Perception is reality after all. If people are exposed to a bias before viewing for themselves, then they are doomed to a biased opinion. Same with films and books, or anything really.

[]: I blame Kaji. He loves to spread misinformation. And wank.             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Wed Jul 14, 2010 5:17 pm NAveryW wrote: It comes from all the misinformation spread in Eva 's early days that won't die because people immediately access it when trying to understand the show better. Their perception of the show is then skewed through what they've read, resulting in more confusion.

[]: Re: I blame Kaji. He loves to spread misinformation. And wan             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 5:20 pm Killer Bee wrote: This. I'm still untangling all the ancient rumors after becoming part of the fandom several months ago; it's a messy process that nevertheless must be done. In doesn't help that, for every person that 'exposes' the rumor for what it is, there's always another person who 'proves' otherwise. It's a never-ending cycle of pain and humiliation. ...and fanwanking. Don't forget fanwanking.

[]:             Quote Post by Tails_night » Wed Jul 14, 2010 8:20 pm The only part that really confused me was when I first started watching it, I was wondering why these destructive beings would be called angels, then I watched the whole show and read some of the manga, and everythign made sense. But beyond that i found it fascinating, far from confusing. I admit, I watch Evangelion with anticipation and admiration, but beyond that I keep my brain outside of fanwanking and fangasming over it.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:30 pm NAveryW wrote: It comes from all the misinformation spread in Eva 's early days that won't die because people immediately access it when trying to understand the show better. Their perception of the show is then skewed through what they've read, resulting in more confusion.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Jul 14, 2010 9:35 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: but how much debatable/questionable material is still left?

[]:             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 10:21 pm I've always thought that the Lore of Evangelion is a little....well, hard. And this turns people off of it early on. These people are probably more physically minded than mentally. They might think if the physical part is screwy, the rest must be even worse

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:08 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Enough to suck money out of people for another decade explaining it.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:24 pm I've always thought that Eva, while being mostly straightforward, is so full of foreshadowing that's mainly inconsequential to what's going on at the current time, but becomes vital to understand what happens later. I think Evangelion would be 'easier' if it tried to remind the audience "Oh yeah, about ten episodes ago, we hinted that this was going to happen. You were paying attention, right?" A lot of the perceived 'difficulty' probably stems from that lack of reminders. Eva wants you to be an active viewer and take note of the things it's not explaining to you, but showing you. A form of storytelling I, incidentally, always found far more interesting and rewording.

[]:             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:26 pm MugwumpHasNoLiver wrote: I've always thought that Eva, while being mostly straightforward, is so full of foreshadowing that's mainly inconsequential to what's going on at the current time, but becomes vital to understand what happens later. I think Evangelion would be 'easier' if it tried to remind the audience "Oh yeah, about ten episodes ago, we hinted that this was going to happen. You were paying attention, right?" A lot of the perceived 'difficulty' probably stems from that lack of reminders. Eva wants you to be an active viewer and take note of the things it's not explaining to you, but showing you. A form of storytelling I, incidentally, always found far more interesting and rewording.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:29 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Not sure what you mean here... Rebuild's profits aren't exactly built around the promise that it will explain the series...

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:34 am Well, I still don't think that has much to do with it. People have to care about something to care about any answers in the first place. I'm guessing if a popular series that wasn't as mysterious as NGE released new stuff people would gobble it up just as much.

[]:             Quote Post by carla » Thu Jul 15, 2010 9:37 am are you kidding me, half this stuff went right over my head the first time i watched the show. granted, i wasn't really looking for anything that complex the first time around, i was mostly just being entertained. but i don't consider myself stupid by any means and let me tell you, when you finish watching a series and the only thing that goes through your head is "...what just happened?", well, it's not a good sign. i rewatched, and rewatched, and i still didn't get much of the subtext. i practically lived off the internet those first few years, i don't know what i would've done without the internet to clarify things for me. a lot of people are okay with vague metaphysical explanations. i'm a curious person, very science-oriented; i like things to make sense and fit properly. i like to ask the whys. and when something sounds off to me, i poke it and poke it and poke it until i figure out a possible reason for it. EVA left a lot of ends hanging on purpose, not to mention that a lot of the information that now seems basic to me was given to us in complementary documents like the CI and some of the video games, which i wouldn't have had access to without the internet. so yeah, i don't think EVA is the type of series you can just watch and "get," at least not in my experience. i think you can get the general feeling of the themes, the characters' motivations and such, but i wouldn't call that a complete understanding. even a decade after my first exposure to it, sometimes i rewatch it and get confused all over again, because i keep noticing things i hadn't noticed before-- but by now i expect that to happen, so it doesn't bother me as much. of course we can say it's easy now , when we've spent the last 10+ years ruminating about it, and discussing it, and nitpicking it. but at the beginning, it was damn hard. i personally just happen to like the challenge. a lot of people don't.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 15, 2010 9:41 pm carla wrote: when you finish watching a series and the only thing that goes through your head is "...what just happened?", well, it's not a good sign.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Jul 15, 2010 10:00 pm Or that the creators completely failed at conveying information to the audience in a meaningful fashion.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Jul 15, 2010 10:03 pm There is a fine line between the two.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu Jul 15, 2010 10:19 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: There is a fine line between the two.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Thu Jul 15, 2010 10:26 pm You can't apply that to every story though.
summary:
[]: The Difficulty of Evangelion             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 4:58 pm Personally, I don't find Eva that "hard" to understand, philosophically, psychologically, and lore-wise, but the main complaint from haters is that it's far too confusing. I can see why, but never had any problems with it myself. What do you think the difficulty of Evangelion stems from?

Article: The Difficulty of Evangelion
 the first time i watched the show. granted, i wasn't really looking for anything that complex the first time around, i was mostly just being entertained. but i don't consider myself stupid by any means and let me tell you, when you finish watching a series and the only thing that goes through your head is "...what just happened?", well, it's not a good sign. i rewatched, and rewatched, and i still didn't get much of the subtext. i practically lived off the internet those first few years, i don't know what i would've done without the internet to clarify things for me. a lot of people are okay with vague metaphysical explanations. i'm a curious person, very science-oriented; i like things to make sense and fit properly. i like to ask the whys. and when something sounds off to me, i poke it and poke it and poke it until i figure out a possible reason for it. EVA left a lot of ends hanging on purpose, not to mention that a lot of the information that now seems basic to me was given to us in complementary documents like the CI and some of the video games, which i wouldn't have had access to without the internet. so yeah, i don't think EVA is the type of series you can just watch and "get," at least not in my experience. i think you can get the general feeling of the themes, the characters' motivations and such, but i wouldn't call that a complete understanding. even a decade after my first exposure to it, sometimes i rewatch it and get confused all over again, because i keep noticing things i hadn't noticed before-- but by now i expect that to happen, so it doesn't bother me as much. of course we can say it's easy now , when we've spent the last 10+ years ruminating about it, and discussing it, and nitpicking it. but at the beginning, it was damn hard. i personally just happen to like the challenge. a lot of people don't.
